brief: GAP/WHY: Catheter ablation is an effective AF treatment but resource-intensive and not uniformly available; unknown how AF ablation utilization varies by geography, race, ethnicity, socioeconomic status; inequitable access in marginalized groups may create/propagate health inequities. DESIGN/SETTING: Observational utilization study using US Medicare Inpatient and Outpatient claims data files, 2016-2019. POPULATION: Medicare fee-for-service beneficiaries with a diagnosis of AF; analysis at ZIP code level. METHOD/COMPARISON: Generalized Estimating Equations (GEE) modeling association between ZIP-code-level racial/ethnic/socioeconomic composition and ZIP-code-level AF catheter ablation rates; exposures per each 10% increase in a demographic group within a ZIP code; outcome = AF ablation rate (patients per 10,000). KEY RESULTS: (1) Each 10% increase in dual-eligible-for-Medicaid patients (poverty marker) per ZIP code = 275 fewer patients per 10,000 underwent AF ablation (P=.0003). (2) After adjusting for dual-eligible status: each 10% increase in Black patients per ZIP code = 618 fewer underwent AF ablation (P<.0001). (3) Each 10% increase in Hispanic patients per ZIP code = 430 fewer underwent AF ablation (P=.002). TAKEAWAY: Significant inequities exist in AF ablation utilization tied to racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic differences; lower utilization concentrated in marginalized populations, with potential to generate and perpetuate health inequities.
